title: Special events keep Peninsula residents busy
summary: Some people get their kicks dressing up in goofy costumes and jumping into an ice-cold bay. And plenty of people enjoy watching such a spectacle, namely the Polar Bear Plunge in Seward. This annual three-day January event draws quite a crowd, not to mention jumpers. And Seward has wrapped quite a festival around this colorful happening. "It started as a one-day event, just jump in the bay, and it grew from there," says Marilyn Reynolds. She handles the marketing and public relations for the event and has three jumps to her credit. "The worst was when it was 34 below chill factor in 1989," she said with a shiver. Part of her job is recruiting jumpers and for 1999, she's got 25 potential candidates already signed up. "This is the first time we've had so many people sign up by September," she…

title: Seward Military Resort
summary: Restaurant, motel, lounge and gift shop "at the heart of the small boat harbor" in Seward, Alaska. PO Box 2147, Seward, Alaska 99664 Phone (907) 224-5238 Fax (907) 224-7024 Spacious, comfortable rooms at affordable prices. Our friendly, knowledgeable staff will assist you in every way possible to make your stay with us a memorable one. Our motel is within the small boat harbor, which is the departure point for all the Kenai Fjord National Park Tour Boats and Fishing Charters. Our restaurant specializes in Fresh Alaska Seafood. Banquet facilities are available for parties and special events. Please call for reservations or email us at: breeze@ptialaska.net breeze@ptialaska.net http://www.AlaskaOne.com/breezeinn/

title: Bear sighting prompts safety warning
summary: Bear sighting prompts safety warning By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter The bears are back in town, and Anchorage residents need to put their bird feeders away and alter their garbage-disposal habits, a state biologist said Friday. A young brown bear was spotted by several people Friday near Kasuun Elementary School on 68th Avenue and was still in the area in the evening, said Rick Sinnott of the state Department of Fish and Game. Sinnott believes the grizzly is one of two young bears seen last summer in lower Hillside neighborhoods eating trash from large garbage containers and from bags residents put out for pickup. Those two brown bears were also observed last fall among trees near Bowman Elementary School in southeast Anchorage. Sinnott also suspects that the other of the two bears is …

title: Video gambling bill to remain in committee
summary: Video gambling bill to remain in committee By DAN JOLING The Associated Press JUNEAU - Critics of a video gambling bill say the biggest beneficiaries would be bar owners, not the nonprofit organizations that are supposed to profit from charitable gaming in Alaska. In testimony Friday before the House Judiciary Committee, they said the bill would possibly reduce the number of charities that benefit. They also said the state should not link gambling to establishments that sell alcohol. Committee Chairman Pete Kott, R-Anchorage, sponsor of House Bill 182, said afterward the measure will not move out of committee this session. "I think it's too big, too late, for any kind of move," Kott said. The hearing began with a presentation by David Hooper, public affairs manager for the Oregon Lottery. …

title: Copyright © 2000 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: UPS told to pay Court finds firm owes fuel tax The Associated Press ANCHORAGE - The Alaska Supreme Court ruled Friday that United Parcel Service must pay state taxes on all fuel pumped onto its planes in Alaska for domestic flights. The express shipping company had claimed that, under the Alaska Motor Fuel Tax Act, it should be taxed only on the amount of fuel it consumed in the state or in Alaska airspace. UPS argued that taxing out-of-state consumption was not right because a state tax can apply only to transactions that are located or take place within the state. But the high court disagreed with that analysis. "Neither law nor logic would support the conclusion that the precise moment of jet-fuel combustion is the only permissible moment for triggering tax liability based on consumptio…

title: Legislator challenges wolf hunt
summary: Legislator challenges wolf hunt Land-shoot amendment fails 10-28 in House By MARTHA BELLISLE Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - The House debate over a bill that all but overturns a citizens initiative banning land-and-shoot wolf hunting shifted Monday from killing wolves to respecting voters' wishes. "Whether I agree or disagree with the initiative doesn't matter," said Rep. Andrew Halcro, an Anchorage Republican who tried to dramatically amend a same-day airborne wolf hunting bill. "The point is I have to respect the people's vote." Land-and-shoot wolf hunting was banned by a 1996 ballot initiative approved by 58.5 percent of voters. The initiative drew 3,542 yes votes in Halcro's district. A rush of pressure from some House members to get Halcro to drop the amendment proved fruitless. "I…

title: Copyright © 2000 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: School bills panned 5 of 69 on list would be built By DAN JOLING The Associated Press JUNEAU - The state House and Senate are considering separate $269.5 million bond packages to repair or build Alaska schools, but some rural legislators say the measures don't go far enough. Senate Bill 310 calls for fixing or building schools with money raised by general obligation bonds, which need voter approval and are paid off over time with the revenue taken in by state government. House Bill 281 proposes using revenue bonds guaranteed with income expected from Alaska's share of the settlement with tobacco companies. Sen. Al Adams, D-Kotzebue, said he is less concerned with how bonds are paid off than what they pay for. The bills call for building only five rural schools on the state Department of Ed…

title: Interior school closed for a week over threats to teacher
summary: Interior school closed for a week over threats to teacher The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - Threats made to a teacher at Cruikshank School in Beaver resulted in the school's closing for a week. The 24 students in kindergarten through high school were sent home March 31, said Ron Yatlin, a member of the Local Schools Advisory Committee. The superintendent then notified the village that the school would be closed until further notice. Troopers Sgt. Ron Wall said the school has been the subject of reports to Alaska State Troopers twice in the past six weeks. Normally the community is peaceful, he said. On Feb. 23, troopers received a report of inappropriate contact between two students at the school, Wall said. A potential charge of second-degree sexual assault against the male student was for…

title: End of beetles in sight; fire danger to last decades
summary: End of beetles in sight; fire danger to last decades By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter Southcentral Alaska has been fighting the spruce bark beetle for better than 10 years and will be dealing with the aftereffects for a long time, forest and firefighting experts say. With millions of acres of spruce trees killed, they said, about the only consolation is that the infestation is going to die out, probably in the next few years. But that won't be the end of it. Southcentral Alaska can expect a generation of wildfire hazards and other long-term effects of the massive spruce die off, specialists said at a spruce bark beetle workshop in Anchorage last weekend. "The ecological impacts could be decades if not a century," said John See, a state forestry coordinator. The impact of the fire danger …
